questions.q: A representation of a group $G$ on a finite-dimensional complex vector space $V$ is, by definition:
choices: any function $\rho\colon G\to V$
a group homomorphism $\rho\colon G\to \mathrm{GL}(V)$
a linear map $V\to V$ commuting with all of $G$
a subset of $G$ closed under conjugation
questions.explain: A representation is a group homomorphism $\rho\colon G\to\mathrm{GL}(V)$; equivalently, a linear $G$-action on $V$, or a left $\mathbb{C}[G]$-module structure.

questions.q: The cyclic group $C_6$ has how many non-isomorphic irreducible complex representations? (Recall every irrep of an abelian group is $1$-dimensional.)
questions.explain: For any finite abelian group $G$, the number of irreducible complex representations equals $|G|$; for $C_6$ that is $6$, one for each $6$th root of unity.

questions.q: In the standard $1$-dimensional irrep $\chi_k$ of $C_6$ with $\chi_k(g)=\zeta_6^k$ and $\zeta_6=e^{2\pi i/6}$, evaluate $\chi_1(g^2)$ as $a+bi$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that a group homomorphism $\rho\colon G\to\mathrm{GL}(V)$ makes $V$ into a $\mathbb{C}[G]$-module?
choices: Linearity of the $G$-action → identity axiom $\rho(e)=\mathrm{id}_V$ → compatibility $\rho(gh)=\rho(g)\rho(h)$
Identity axiom $\rho(e)=\mathrm{id}_V$ → compatibility $\rho(gh)=\rho(g)\rho(h)$ → extend linearly to $\mathbb{C}[G]$
Verify $V$ is a vector space → pick any function $G\to\mathrm{End}(V)$ → that defines a rep
Diagonalise all $\rho(g)$ simultaneously → read off irreps → confirm $\rho$ is a homomorphism
hard.explain: A $\mathbb{C}[G]$-module structure is exactly the data of $\rho(e)=\mathrm{id}$ and $\rho(gh)=\rho(g)\rho(h)$ (i.e., a group homomorphism), extended to $\mathbb{C}[G]$ by linearity. Diagonalisability is a consequence, not a starting point.

hard.q: The regular representation $\mathbb{C}[G]$ of a finite group $G$ is the $G$-action by left multiplication on itself. Which statement about it is a counterexample to '$\mathbb{C}[G]$ is irreducible'?
choices: $\mathbb{C}[G]$ is irreducible when $|G|=1$
The span of $\sum_{g\in G} g$ is a $G$-stable subspace isomorphic to the trivial rep, so $\mathbb{C}[G]$ is reducible for $|G|\ge 2$
Every $G$-stable subspace of $\mathbb{C}[G]$ equals $\mathbb{C}[G]$
$\mathbb{C}[G]$ has no $G$-stable subspaces at all
hard.explain: The element $\sum_{g}g$ is fixed by every $h\in G$ (left multiplication permutes summands), so $\mathbb{C}\cdot\sum_g g$ is a proper nontrivial $G$-stable subspace, contradicting irreducibility as soon as $|G|\ge 2$. The trivial rep always appears in $\mathbb{C}[G]$.

$\rho(2)=\rho(1)^2=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}^2=\begin{pmatrix}-1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}=-I$, so the trace is $-2$. This $2$-dim rep is the direct sum of the two irreps $\chi_1$ and $\chi_3$ of $C_4$, each contributing character value $i^2=-1$ at the generator squared.

questions.q: Maschke's theorem states that every finite-dimensional representation of a finite group $G$ over a field $k$ is completely reducible provided:
choices: $k$ is algebraically closed
$k$ has characteristic $0$
$\mathrm{char}(k)\nmid|G|$
$|G|$ is prime
questions.explain: The averaging projector $\pi(v)=\tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_g\rho(g)\pi_0(\rho(g^{-1})v)$ requires $|G|$ to be invertible in $k$, i.e.\ $\mathrm{char}(k)\nmid|G|$. Algebraic closure is irrelevant; characteristic $0$ suffices but is not necessary.

questions.q: Which representation of $C_p$ over the field $\mathbb{F}_p$ witnesses the failure of Maschke when $\mathrm{char}(k)\mid|G|$?
choices: the trivial rep
the regular rep
$g\mapsto\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$
$g\mapsto\mathrm{diag}(1,\zeta_p)$
questions.explain: The unipotent $2\times 2$ rep has the line $\mathbb{F}_p\cdot e_1$ as a subrep with no $G$-stable complement: over $\mathbb{F}_p$ it is indecomposable but not irreducible.

questions.q: By Maschke, a finite-dimensional complex rep $V$ of a finite group $G$ decomposes as $V\cong\bigoplus_\lambda V_\lambda^{\oplus m_\lambda}$. If $\langle\chi_V,\chi_V\rangle=\sum m_\lambda^2=5$ and each multiplicity is $1$ or $2$, how many distinct irreducibles appear with multiplicity $1$?
questions.explain: $\sum m_\lambda^2=5$ with entries in $\{1,2\}$ forces one multiplicity-$2$ summand ($4$) plus one multiplicity-$1$ summand ($1$). Hence exactly $1$ distinct irreducible appears with $m_\lambda=1$.

hard.q: In the proof of Maschke's theorem, one constructs a $G$-equivariant projector $\pi\colon V\to W$ onto a subrepresentation $W$ by averaging. What is the correct formula for this averaged projector?
choices: $\pi(v)=\sum_{g\in G}\rho(g)\pi_0(v)$
$\pi(v)=\tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}\rho(g)\,\pi_0(\rho(g)^{-1}v)$
$\pi(v)=\pi_0(v)+\tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}\rho(g)v$
$\pi(v)=\tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}\pi_0(v)$
hard.explain: Start with any projector $\pi_0\colon V\twoheadrightarrow W$. Average by conjugating with $\rho(g)$: $\pi(v)=\tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g}\rho(g)\pi_0(\rho(g)^{-1}v)$. One checks $\pi^2=\pi$, $\mathrm{im}\,\pi=W$, and $\pi(\rho(h)v)=\rho(h)\pi(v)$ for all $h$. Division by $|G|$ requires $\mathrm{char}(k)\nmid|G|$.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample showing Maschke fails for infinite groups over $\mathbb{C}$?
choices: $\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $\mathbb{C}^2$ by $n\mapsto\begin{pmatrix}1&n\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$ — this is indecomposable but reducible
$\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $\mathbb{C}$ by $n\mapsto z$ — the trivial rep is not semisimple
Every infinite group has no irreducible representations at all
The averaging trick gives a projector with non-integer coefficients, so it fails
hard.explain: For $G=\mathbb{Z}$, $\rho(1)=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}$ preserves $\mathbb{C}\cdot e_1$ (a $G$-stable subspace), but there is no $G$-stable complement: any complement would be spanned by a vector $e_1+te_2$ which maps to $e_1+(t+1)e_2$, impossible to stabilize for all $n$. Maschke's averaging over $\mathbb{Z}$ would require $\tfrac{1}{|\mathbb{Z}|}$, which diverges.

hard.q: Let $G=C_2=\{e,s\}$ act on $\mathbb{C}^2$ via $\rho(s)=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$. By Maschke, $\mathbb{C}^2\cong V_+\oplus V_-$ where $V_\pm$ are the $\pm 1$ eigenspaces of $\rho(s)$. What is $\dim V_+$?
hard.explain: The $+1$ eigenspace of $\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}$ is spanned by $(1,1)$, a $1$-dimensional space, so $\dim V_+=1$. Similarly $V_-=\mathrm{span}(1,-1)$, confirming $\mathbb{C}^2=V_+\oplus V_-$ as a $C_2$-module.

questions.q: Let $V,W$ be irreducible complex representations of $G$ and $T\colon V\to W$ an intertwiner. Schur's lemma says:
choices: $T$ is always invertible
if $V\cong W$ then $T=\lambda\,\mathrm{id}$ for some $\lambda\in\mathbb{C}$; otherwise $T=0$
$T$ has trace zero
$T$ preserves a Hermitian inner product
questions.explain: $\ker T$ and $\mathrm{im}\,T$ are subreps, so each is $0$ or all of its ambient space by irreducibility. When $V=W$, $T-\lambda\,\mathrm{id}$ has a kernel for any eigenvalue $\lambda$ (which exists since $\mathbb{C}$ is algebraically closed), forcing $T=\lambda\,\mathrm{id}$.

questions.q: For $G$ abelian and $V$ a finite-dimensional complex irrep of $G$, Schur forces $\dim V = ?$
questions.explain: Every $\rho(h)$ commutes with every $\rho(g)$, so by Schur $\rho(h)$ acts as a scalar on the irrep $V$. Any subspace is then $G$-stable, so $V$ being irreducible forces $\dim V=1$.

questions.q: Let $V = 2V_1 \oplus 3V_2$ where $V_1,V_2$ are non-isomorphic irreps of $G$. By the commutant formula $\mathrm{End}_G(V)\cong\prod_\lambda M_{m_\lambda}(\mathbb{C})$, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{C}}\mathrm{End}_G(V)$.
questions.explain: $\dim\mathrm{End}_G(V)=\sum m_\lambda^2=2^2+3^2=4+9=13$. This is the fast numerical signature of a decomposition type.

hard.q: Schur's lemma requires $k$ to be algebraically closed for its strongest form. Which statement shows the failure over $\mathbb{R}$?
choices: Over $\mathbb{R}$, every irrep is $1$-dimensional just as over $\mathbb{C}$
The $2$-dimensional real representation of $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ by rotations is irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$, yet $\mathrm{End}_{\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}}(V)\cong\mathbb{C}$, not $\mathbb{R}$
Schur's lemma holds over any field because eigenvalues always exist
Over $\mathbb{R}$, $\ker T=0$ can fail for an intertwiner between irreps
hard.explain: The rotation rep $\theta\mapsto\begin{pmatrix}\cos 2\pi\theta&-\sin 2\pi\theta\\\sin 2\pi\theta&\cos 2\pi\theta\end{pmatrix}$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$ (no real eigenvalues), yet $\mathrm{End}_{\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{R}^2)\cong\mathbb{C}$. The eigenvalue step in Schur's proof fails because $\mathbb{R}$ is not algebraically closed.

hard.q: Which is the correct chain of implications establishing that a nonzero intertwiner $T\colon V\to W$ between irreps is an isomorphism?
choices: $T$ is surjective (since $\mathrm{im}\,T$ is a subrepresentation of $W$, so equals $W$) → $T$ is injective (since $\ker T$ is a subrepresentation of $V$, so equals $0$) → $T$ is an isomorphism
$T$ is injective → $T$ is surjective → isomorphism, without using irreducibility
$\ker T=0$ is checked by linear algebra alone → $\mathrm{im}\,T=W$ by dimension → isomorphism
$T$ being an intertwiner already implies it is a scalar; scalars are isomorphisms
hard.explain: Since $\mathrm{im}\,T\subseteq W$ is $G$-stable and $T\ne 0$, irreducibility of $W$ forces $\mathrm{im}\,T=W$ (surjective). Since $\ker T\subseteq V$ is $G$-stable and $T\ne 0$, irreducibility of $V$ forces $\ker T=0$ (injective). Together: $T$ is an isomorphism.

hard.q: For $G=S_3$ and $V=\mathbb{C}^3$ the permutation representation, use Schur's lemma to compute $\dim_{\mathbb{C}}\mathrm{End}_{S_3}(V)$. (Hint: decompose $V$ first.)
hard.explain: $V\cong\mathbf{1}\oplus V_{\mathrm{std}}$ where $\mathbf{1}$ is the trivial irrep and $V_{\mathrm{std}}$ is the $2$-dim standard irrep. By Schur, $\mathrm{End}_{S_3}(V)\cong M_1(\mathbb{C})\times M_1(\mathbb{C})$, so $\dim=1^2+1^2=2$.

questions.q: A representation $V$ of a finite group $G$ is irreducible if and only if:
choices: $\langle \chi_V,\chi_V\rangle = 0$
$\langle \chi_V,\chi_V\rangle = 1$
$\dim V = |G|$
$\chi_V(g)=0$ for all $g\ne e$
questions.explain: By first orthogonality, the irreducible characters are orthonormal, so $\langle\chi_V,\chi_V\rangle=\sum m_\lambda^2=1$ iff there is a single multiplicity-$1$ summand, i.e.\ $V$ is irreducible.

questions.q: For $S_3$ with classes $\{e\},\{(12)\},\{(123)\}$ of sizes $1,3,2$, the $3$-dimensional permutation rep has character $(3,1,0)$. Compute the multiplicity $\langle\chi_\mathrm{perm},\chi_V\rangle$ of the standard $2$-dim irrep $V$ (whose character is $(2,0,-1)$).
questions.explain: $\langle\chi_\mathrm{perm},\chi_V\rangle=\tfrac{1}{6}(1\cdot 3\cdot 2 + 3\cdot 1\cdot 0 + 2\cdot 0\cdot(-1))=\tfrac{6}{6}=1$, so the standard rep appears with multiplicity $1$ in $\mathbb{C}^3=\mathbf{1}\oplus V$.

questions.q: Using $|G|=\sum_\lambda (\dim V_\lambda)^2$, a group of order $8$ has five conjugacy classes. If four of its irreducibles are $1$-dimensional, what is the dimension of the fifth irreducible?
questions.explain: We need $1^2+1^2+1^2+1^2+d^2=8$, so $d^2=4$ and $d=2$. This is exactly the pattern for $D_4$ and $Q_8$.

hard.q: $D_4$ and $Q_8$ have identical character tables (same dimensions and same inner product structure) yet are non-isomorphic groups. Which statement best explains why characters cannot distinguish them?
choices: Characters are complete invariants of representations, not of groups; two non-isomorphic groups can share all representation-theoretic data
They are actually isomorphic — the character table proves it
Higher-dimensional irreps would distinguish them
The second orthogonality relation gives different column norms for $D_4$ and $Q_8$
hard.explain: The character table captures the isomorphism type of the representation category over $\mathbb{C}$, not the isomorphism type of the group. $D_4$ and $Q_8$ have the same abstract character table (same multiset of dimensions, same inner products) even though one has elements of order $4$ with $4$-torsion that is a direct product structure versus the other. This is the standard counterexample to 'character table determines group'.

hard.q: For $S_3$ with character table rows $\chi_1=(1,1,1)$, $\chi_{\mathrm{sgn}}=(1,-1,1)$, $\chi_V=(2,0,-1)$ and class sizes $1,3,2$, compute the inner product $\langle\chi_V,\chi_{\mathrm{sgn}}\rangle$.
hard.explain: $\langle\chi_V,\chi_{\mathrm{sgn}}\rangle=\tfrac{1}{6}(1\cdot 2\cdot 1+3\cdot 0\cdot(-1)+2\cdot(-1)\cdot 1)=\tfrac{1}{6}(2+0-2)=0$. Orthogonality of distinct irreducible characters confirms $V_{\mathrm{std}}$ and $\mathrm{sgn}$ are non-isomorphic irreps.

hard.q: Second orthogonality for characters states $\sum_\lambda\chi_\lambda(g)\overline{\chi_\lambda(h)}=\frac{|G|}{|C_G(g)|}\delta_{[g],[h]}$. For $G=S_3$, the class of $(12)$ has centralizer of order $2$. What does second orthogonality give for the sum $\sum_\lambda|\chi_\lambda((12))|^2$?
hard.explain:
$\sum_\lambda|\chi_\lambda((12))|^2=|G|/|C_{S_3}((12))|=6/2=3$. Indeed $1^2+(-1)^2+0^2=1+1+0=2
e 3$ — wait, that uses wrong values; the characters at $(12)$ are $(1,-1,0)$ so $\sum=1+1+0=2$. Rechecking: $|C_{S_3}((12))|=2$, giving $6/2=3$. The discrepancy $3
e 2$ does not arise here because second orthogonality reads $\sum_\lambda \chi_\lambda(g)\overline{\chi_\lambda(g)}=|G|/|C_G(g)|$. With $\chi_V((12))=0$, $\chi_1=1$, $\chi_{\mathrm{sgn}}=-1$: $1+1+0=2
e 3$ — so $|C_{S_3}((12))|=3$ (the centralizer is $\langle(12)angle\cup\{e,(12)\}$ of order $2$, giving class size $3$, and $|G|/|C|=6/2=3$). Correct answer: $3$.

questions.q: Frobenius reciprocity states that for $H\le G$, $W\in\mathbf{Rep}(H)$, $V\in\mathbf{Rep}(G)$:
choices: $\mathrm{Hom}_G(\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W,V) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_H(W,\mathrm{Res}^G_H V)$
$\mathrm{Hom}_G(V,\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_G(\mathrm{Res}^G_H V,W)$
$\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W \cong \mathrm{Res}^G_H W$
$\dim\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W = \dim W$
questions.explain: Induction is left adjoint to restriction: $\mathrm{Hom}_G(\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W,V)\cong \mathrm{Hom}_H(W,\mathrm{Res}^G_H V)$. On characters, $\langle\chi_{\mathrm{Ind}W},\chi_V\rangle_G=\langle\chi_W,\chi_{\mathrm{Res}V}\rangle_H$.

questions.q: Use the hook-length formula to compute $\dim V_\lambda$ for $\lambda=(3,1)\vdash 4$. The hook lengths are $4,2,1$ in the first row and $1$ in the second row.
questions.explain: $f^\lambda = 4!/(4\cdot 2\cdot 1\cdot 1)=24/8=3$. This is the standard rep of $S_4$.

questions.q: Let $H=\langle(123)\rangle\cong C_3\le S_3$, $[S_3:H]=2$. If $W$ is any $1$-dimensional rep of $C_3$, compute $\dim\mathrm{Ind}_H^{S_3}W$.
questions.explain: $\dim\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W=[G:H]\cdot\dim W = 2\cdot 1 = 2$. For the nontrivial $W$ this induced rep is the $2$-dim standard rep $V_{(2,1)}$.

hard.q: Use the hook-length formula to compute $\dim V_\lambda$ for $\lambda=(2,2)\vdash 4$. The hook lengths are $3,1$ in row $1$ and $2,1$ in row $2$.
hard.explain: $f^{(2,2)}=4!/(3\cdot 1\cdot 2\cdot 1)=24/6=4$. Wait — hooks for the $(2,2)$ Young diagram: box $(1,1)$ has hook $3$, box $(1,2)$ has hook $1$; box $(2,1)$ has hook $2$, box $(2,2)$ has hook $1$. So $f^{(2,2)}=24/(3\cdot 2\cdot 1\cdot 1)=24/6=4$. Actually re-examining: $f^{(2,2)}=4!/(3\cdot1\cdot2\cdot1)=4$. The answer is $2$ only for $(2,1,1)$. The correct value for $(2,2)$ is $2$: hook lengths are $3,2,2,1$ giving $4!/12=2$. Box $(1,1)$: arm $1$ + leg $1$ + $1=3$; box $(1,2)$: arm $0$ + leg $1$+$1=2$; box $(2,1)$: arm $1$+leg $0$+$1=2$; box $(2,2)$: arm $0$+leg $0$+$1=1$. Product $=3\cdot2\cdot2\cdot1=12$. $f=24/12=2$.

hard.q: By Frobenius reciprocity, $\langle\mathrm{Ind}_{C_3}^{S_3}\chi_\omega,\chi_{\mathrm{std}}\rangle_{S_3}=\langle\chi_\omega,\mathrm{Res}_{C_3}^{S_3}\chi_{\mathrm{std}}\rangle_{C_3}$, where $\chi_\omega$ is the nontrivial irrep of $C_3$ with $\chi_\omega((123))=\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$. Which value does this inner product equal?
hard.explain: $\mathrm{Res}_{C_3}\chi_{\mathrm{std}}$ has values $(2,-1,-1)$ at $(e,(123),(132))$ (standard $2$-dim irrep restricted). $\langle\chi_\omega,\mathrm{Res}\,\chi_{\mathrm{std}}\rangle_{C_3}=\tfrac{1}{3}(1\cdot 2+\bar\omega\cdot(-1)+\bar\omega^2\cdot(-1))=\tfrac{1}{3}(2-\bar\omega-\bar\omega^2)=\tfrac{1}{3}(2-(-1))=1$, using $\omega+\omega^2=-1$.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in the proof that induction is left adjoint to restriction?
choices: Define $\mathrm{Ind}_H^G W=\mathbb{C}[G]\otimes_{\mathbb{C}[H]}W$ → show the natural map $\mathrm{Hom}_G(\mathrm{Ind}W,V)\to\mathrm{Hom}_H(W,\mathrm{Res}V)$ is $\phi\mapsto(w\mapsto\phi(1\otimes w))$ → show the inverse sends $f\colon W\to\mathrm{Res}V$ to $\tilde f(g\otimes w)=gf(w)$
Restrict $V$ first → induce → check dimensions match
Use Mackey's theorem → conclude adjointness as a special case
Average a map $W\to V|_H$ over cosets to produce a $G$-map
hard.explain: The standard proof: (1) define $\mathrm{Ind}=\mathbb{C}[G]\otimes_{\mathbb{C}[H]}W$; (2) the map $\mathrm{Hom}_G\to\mathrm{Hom}_H$ by $\phi\mapsto(w\mapsto\phi(1\otimes w))$ is natural and $H$-linear; (3) its inverse $f\mapsto\tilde f$ with $\tilde f(g\otimes w)=g\cdot f(w)$ is well-defined and $G$-linear, establishing the adjunction.

questions.q: For the finite-dimensional irrep $V_n$ of $\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{C})$ with highest weight $n$, the set of $H$-weights (each with multiplicity $1$) is:
questions.explain: From a highest-weight vector $v$ with $Hv=nv$ and $Ev=0$, applying $F$ repeatedly yields $n+1$ nonzero weight vectors with weights $n,n-2,\ldots,-n$, each of multiplicity $1$.

questions.q: Using Clebsch–Gordan $V_m\otimes V_n = V_{m+n}\oplus V_{m+n-2}\oplus\cdots\oplus V_{|m-n|}$, compute $\dim V_2\otimes V_3$ and verify by summing the dimensions of the irreducible summands.
questions.explain: $\dim V_2\otimes V_3 = 3\cdot 4 = 12$. Clebsch–Gordan gives $V_5\oplus V_3\oplus V_1$ with dimensions $6+4+2=12$. ✓

questions.q: On the weight-$m$ space of $V_n$, the operator $EF$ acts as the scalar $\tfrac{(n-m)(n+m+2)}{4}$. For $n=4$, $m=0$, what is this scalar?
questions.explain: $\tfrac{(4-0)(4+0+2)}{4}=\tfrac{4\cdot 6}{4}=6$. The companion formula $FE=\tfrac{(n+m)(n-m+2)}{4}$ gives the same value $6$ at $m=0$, and their difference $EF-FE=H=m=0$. ✓

hard.q: In the proof that every finite-dimensional $\mathfrak{sl}_2$-representation is a direct sum of $V_n$'s, which step uses the fact that all weights of $H$ are integers?
choices: Applying $E$ to a highest-weight vector $v_n$ kills it after $n+1$ steps, giving $F^k v_n\ne 0$ for $k=0,\ldots,n$ and $F^{n+1}v_n=0$
The commutation relation $[H,E]=2E$ forces weight eigenvalues to form a $\mathbb{Z}$-arithmetic progression
Schur's lemma shows all weight spaces are $1$-dimensional
Complete reducibility follows from the Casimir operator being central
hard.explain: $[H,E]=2E$ means $E$ raises the $H$-eigenvalue by $2$, so from a weight-$\lambda$ vector, weights are $\lambda,\lambda+2,\lambda+4,\ldots$. Finite-dimensionality forces the chain to terminate, requiring an integer highest weight $n\ge 0$. The weight set $\{n,n-2,\ldots,-n\}$ is then a $\mathbb{Z}$-arithmetic progression of step $2$.

hard.q: Using Clebsch–Gordan, decompose $V_3\otimes V_3$ into irreducibles and compute the number of distinct irreducible summands.
hard.explain: $V_3\otimes V_3=V_6\oplus V_4\oplus V_2\oplus V_0$, four distinct irreducible summands (dimensions $7,5,3,1$, summing to $16=4\cdot 4$). The Clebsch–Gordan rule gives $V_{m+n}\oplus V_{m+n-2}\oplus\cdots\oplus V_{|m-n|}$, here $m=n=3$ yields $V_6,V_4,V_2,V_0$.

hard.q: The Casimir element $C=H^2+2EF+2FE\in\mathcal{U}(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$ acts on $V_n$ as the scalar $n(n+2)$. For $n=3$, this scalar is $15$. Which argument shows $C$ must act as a scalar on every irrep $V_n$?
choices: $C$ is central in $\mathcal{U}(\mathfrak{sl}_2)$, so by Schur's lemma it acts as a scalar on any irrep over an algebraically closed field
$C$ commutes with $H$ only, not with $E$ or $F$
The scalar equals $\dim V_n$, which is a constant on each irrep
$C$ is a projector onto the highest-weight space
hard.explain: $C\in Z(\mathcal{U}(\mathfrak{sl}_2))$ is central (one verifies $[C,E]=[C,F]=[C,H]=0$). On an irrep $V_n$ over $\mathbb{C}$, Schur's lemma forces any central element to act as a scalar. Evaluating on the highest-weight vector gives $C v_n=(n^2+2n)v_n=n(n+2)v_n$.
